title: Edison and the Phonograph
creator: Edison
description: Thomas Edison poses with his second model phonograph in 1878. He claimed it was the first device that could record sound and play it back. The truth is more complicated than that.
subject: Edison; sound
source: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs
image_alt_text: [Thomas Edison, full-length portrait, seated, facing front, with phonograph]

title: Phonautograph
creator: Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville
description: The phonautograph is the earliest sound recording device we know of. One would speak or sing into the horn, and the device would transcribe the sounds into markings on coal-blackened paper or glass. However, Scott intended for people to read the markings off the device and translate them to sounds, and did not design the device to play recordings back. The first known recording by a human (Scott singing Au Claire de la Lune in 1860) was not actually heard by anyone until March 2008.
subject: sound; transcription
source: Édouard-Léon Scott de Martinville (1817–1879), Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_alt_text: Black and white diagram of a phonautograph.

title: Kinetoscope
creator: William Kennedy Laurie Dickson
description: The kinetoscope was claimed to be the first motion picture projector by Edison, and he claimed credit for his invention. The reality is that he thought of the idea, told his employee WIlliam Dickson to get on it with his team, and then claimed the credit for its creation when it went public.
subject: motion; edison
source: Albert Tissandier, Public domain, via Wikimedia Commons
image_alt_text: Black and white diagram of a kinetoscope.

title: The Electricity Building
creator: Henry Van Brunt, Frank Maynard Howe
description: The Electricity Building at the Columbian Exposition was a massive structure lit up with myriad light displays inside, a grand monument to mankind’s collaborative excellence. By all accounts, it was a genuinely dazzling sight. The lighting of the 1893 Chicago World’s Fair was a battleground for the rivalry between Thomas Edison and J.P. Morgan, and Nikola Tesla and George Westinghouse; this building represented Tesla and Westinghouse’s victory, and helped showcase the impending supremacy of AC (alternating current) over DC (direct current) electricity. Like most structures built for the 1893 Chicago World’s Fair, the Electricity Building was created with materials that weren’t meant to last, and it was demolished after the fair was over.
subject: light; world’s fair
source: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs
image_alt_text: Electrical building. World's Columbian exposition

title: [Map of the upper Great Plains and Rocky Mountains region]
creator: De Smet, Pierre-Jean, 1801-1873
description: This map represents an earlier moment before Native borders were heavily reduced by U.S. policies. The land appears more continuous and less restricted, showing how Indigenous territories once covered large regions of the West. In the context of this exhibit, this image serves as a starting point helping viewers see what Native land looked like before it began shrinking. It highlights how borders were not originally confined to small, rigid spaces, emphasizing the scale of land that would later be lost.
subject: Native American land; Western U.S.; pre-allotment; Indigenous geography; cultural landscapes
source: Library of Congress Geography and Map Division
image_alt_text: Map of the upper Great Plains and Rocky Mountains region

title: Map showing Indian reservations within the limits of the United States compiled under the direction of D.M. Browning, commissioner of Indian Affairs
publisher: United States. Office of Indian Affairs
description:
The map shows a major shift in Native borders as they begin to shrink into smaller, defined reservation spaces. By 1893, Indigenous land has already been significantly reduced and reorganized by the U.S. government. Compared to earlier maps, Native territories are no longer large and continuous but broken into scattered sections. This item helps viewers see the process of shrinking in action, as land is confined and controlled, marking a transition from widespread homelands to restricted reservations.

-Madison Viveiros

Locates all the lands reserved for Native American tribes in 1893. Includes insets of Alaska, the Mission Indian Reservation in California, and the N.E. corner of Indian Territory.

- Nicholas Bateman
subject: reservations; shrinking land; 1893; fragmentation; federal control
image_alt_text: Map of the United States depicting Indian Reservations in orange.

title: Map of Tripp County, South Dakota showing allotment, allotment numbers, and deeded lands published by Samuel Chilton and C.W. Owen.
creator: Unknown
description: This map  represents the final stage of Native border shrinking, where land is not only reduced but divided into small, individual plots. The grid pattern shows how the Dawes Act broke apart reservations, opening Native land to white settlers through lotteries. Instead of even small shared territories, the land is now fragmented into rigid squares, erasing traditional boundaries completely. This item demonstrates how Native borders didn’t just shrink—they were systematically broken apart, leaving little land under Indigenous control.
subject: Dawes Act; allotment; shrinking borders; land division; grid system; loss
image_alt_text: Map of Tripp County, South Dakota showing the grid of settlement allotments.

title: Bird's eye view of the World's Columbian Exposition
description: An illustration of the birds-eye view over the temporary exhibition and the greater city of Chicago in the background, creating an illusion of Chicago, presenting it as a city of wonder, when in reality it was a very optimistic and not particularly realistic depiction of how the city actually was for its time, with it being drawn in a particular way that clearly is meant to harken back to prior worlds fairs, rather than trying something new.
subject: World Fair; 1893
image_alt_text: Chromolithographic bird's-eye view of the World's Columbian Exposition in Chigaco.

title: Exposition Universelle
description: One of the earlier world fairs that directly inspired the American exhibitions, the map itself is a bird's-eye view of the fair, with postcards and other illustrations showing off only French culture, despite it supposedly being a world fair celebrating everyone. Which can be seen occurring again in the American Columbian Exposition just a couple of years later, starting a trend.
subject: World Fair; 1889; European
image_alt_text: Chromolithographic bird's-eye view of the Exposition Universelle de Paris in 1889.

title: Shell presents the only map of the Fair that tells you how much time you need to go where you want, see what you want!

subject: World Fair; 1965; New York
image_alt_text: Site map of the 1964 World's Fair in New York.

title: A Map of the New York 1940 World’s Fair

description: The layout of the World's Fair, with a focus on American culture, particularly of the government, with a purposeful exclusion of the rest of the world due to the isolationist policy of the age. With the layout being very different from other world fairs, adding another layer of detachment from Europe and tradition occurring at this world fair.
subject: World Fair; 1940; New York
image_alt_text: Site map of the 1940 World's Fair in New York City.

title: Map showing Indian reservations within the limits of the United States compiled under the direction of the Hon. W.A. Jones, Commissioner of Indian Affairs
publisher: United States. Bureau of Indian Affairs
description: From early colonial times, Europeans often removed native peoples from lands they wanted to occupy. In the 1830's, the Indian Removal Act marked the systematic U.S. federal policy to forcibly remove Native populations from European populated areas. Between 1887 and 1934, various governmental acts continued the removal of native peoples from their lands. This map is one of several versions showing the locations of reservations.
subject: reservation; American Indian; United States
image_alt_text: Map of the United States showing Indian Reservation territories.

title: Indians of the U.S.A.
creator: Jefferson, Louise E.
description: Pictorial map showing locations of Native American tribes in the United States and southeastern Canada. Includes an additional vignette giving historical facts of interest for Native American and Western American history.
subject: American Indian; United States

title: The Buffalo 92nd Infantry Division: a combat paper for combat men. v. 4 (no.10), June 17, 1944.
creator: United States Army Infantry Division, 92nd Special Service Section
description: Official newsletter of the 92nd Infantry Division known as the Buffalo Soldiers who were a segregated military division during World War II. The weekly newsletter kept the soldiers informed by publishing entertaining and educational columns in order to bolster confidence and morale while keeping a record of their experiences. One issue's front cover features the unit's boxing competitions. Most of the newsletters were published "somewhere in Italy" and also featured a Silver Star Roll of Honor for 31 recipients. The division participated in the Italy campaign. The logo was an American buffalo that was used in the 19th century by African-American troops.
image_alt_text: Issue of "The Buffalo" newsletter featuring pictures of boxers on the cover.

title: A Flier Announcing the March on Washington in 1963
creator: March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom; Bayard Rustin; Cleveland Robinson

subject: Civil Rights Movement; African American; Social Politics; Activism; Protest
source: Collection of the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture
image_alt_text: Poster advertising the March on Washington.
object_transcript: An Appeal to You from / Mathew Ahmann, Eugene Carson Blake, James Farmer, Martin Luther King, Jr., John Lewis, Isaiah Minkoff, A. Philip Randolph, Walter Reuther, Roy Wilkins, and Whitney Young / to MARCH on / WASHINGTON / WEDNESDAY AUGUST 28, 1963 / America faces a crisis... / Millions of Negroes are denied freedom / Millions of citizens, black and white, are unemployed... / We demand: - Meaningful Civil Rights Laws / Full and Fair Employment / Massive Federal Works Program / Decent Housing / The Right to Vote / Adequate Integrated Education / In your community, groups are mobilizing for the March. You can get information on how to go to Washington by calling civil rights organizations, religion organizations, trade unions, fraternal organizations and youth groups. / Nathional Office - / March on Washington for Jobs and Freedom / 170 West 130 Street New York 27 FI 8-1900 / Cleveland Robinson / Chairman, Administrative committee / Bayard Rusin / Deputy Director

title: Destruction of tea at Boston Harbor[New York]
description: This illustration depicts colonial Americans throwing tea into the Boston harbor, one of the most well known and oldest examples of American Protest. Members of the protest dressed as Mohawk Indians to disguise themselves as to avoid punishment for treason.
subject: Boston Tea Party; Colonial America; Activism; Protest
source: Library of Congress Prints and Photographs
image_alt_text: Lithographic print depicting the Boston Tea Party with Bostonians celebrating on docks while others, dressed as Native Americans, are on a ship throwing crates of tea into the harbor.

title: Declaration of The Return of Indian Land
creator: Indians of All Tribes
description: Proclamation read by Richard Oakes to the press and government officials at the recently destroyed American Indian Center in San Francisco. The statement was read 11 days before Oakes led 89 Native Americans on boat to Alcatraz, where a 19-month-long occupation and protest began.
subject: Alcatraz Occupation; Native American Land Rights; Activism; Protest
image_alt_text: Declaration handwritten on stretched animal hide.
